scenario: 🎨 Hobbies and Interests — Asking: 'What do you do for fun?'
text:
🎧 YouGlish
To ask someone about their hobbies in a casual conversation, say: {{c1::"What do you do for fun when you're not working?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'wʌ·dʒə duː fər fʌn wɛ·njər nɑt wər·kɪŋ?'
Rules applied:
• what do you → /wʌ·dʒə/ (Yod Coalescence: /d/ + /j/ → /dʒ/ + schwa reduction)
• for → /fər/ (schwa reduction)
• when you're → /wɛ·njər/ (linking consonant to glide /j/)

🧠 Avoid asking 'What are your hobbies?' as it sounds like an interview question. 'What do you do for fun?' is much more conversational.
usage:
• "What do you do for fun on the weekends?"
  • "So, what do you do for fun? Do you hike?"
spanish: '¿Qué haces para divertirte cuando no estás trabajando?' — Pregunta natural sobre pasatiempos. En inglés rápido, 'what do you' colapsa a 'wah-juh', 'for' se reduce a 'fer' y 'when you're' se pronuncia 'wen-jer'.

scenario: 🎨 Hobbies and Interests — Expressing Passion: 'I'm really into...'
text:
🎧 YouGlish
To express that you enjoy a hobby, use: {{c1::"I'm really into photography lately." or "I've been getting into bouldering."}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'aɪm rɪ·li ɪn·tə...'
• into → /ɪn·tə/ (schwa reduction on 'to')
• getting into → /gɛ·ɾɪ·ŋɪn·tə/ (Flap T on 'getting' + linking /ŋ/ to 'into')

🧠 'I'm into [X]' is the standard colloquial replacement for 'I like [X]'. 'I've been getting into [X]' implies it is a new interest.
usage:
• "I'm really into cooking Italian food lately."
  • "I've been getting into running since the spring started."
spanish: 'Me gusta mucho la fotografía últimamente.' / 'He estado metiéndome en la escalada.' — Expresar interés de forma nativa. 'I'm into' = me gusta/interesa. Fonéticamente, la preposición 'to' se debilita a schwa y 'getting' usa Flap T.
